comments.post_body:
I’m building big things while trying not to miss small yet precious moments.
I live by one principle: 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐝𝐚𝐲 𝐢𝐧 𝐟𝐫𝐨𝐧𝐭 𝐨𝐟 𝐦𝐞, 𝐰𝐢𝐧 𝐢𝐭, 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐧 𝐫𝐞𝐩𝐞𝐚𝐭.

Right now, my life runs on parallel tracks.
- A full-time job where I put my best foot forward, staying hands-on with AI evolution.
- A real estate business scaling towards a dream I’ve carried growing up: Belle Vie Estate riverfront estate designed for private multi-day celebrations.
- Two young kids (6 and 3) who need presence and quality time, not leftovers.
- Showing up for parents, siblings, and family.
- Planning financially for a secure future.
- And in between all those: bedtime stories, school projects, after school clubs, healthy meals, hikes, workouts, summer planning, list could go on and on.

Twenty-four hours is painfully short at this stage.
But I don’t overthink “balance.” I run one day at a time.

My rule is simple: 𝐬𝐭𝐚𝐫𝐭 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐝𝐚𝐲 𝐰𝐢𝐭𝐡 3 𝐨𝐮𝐭𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐬 𝐭𝐨 𝐚𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐞𝐯𝐞 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐜𝐮𝐭 𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐲𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐝𝐨𝐞𝐬𝐧’𝐭 𝐬𝐞𝐫𝐯𝐞 𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐦.
I say No to everyone and everything that doesn’t fit. No explanations. No guilt spirals. Just a clear tradeoff.
This rule doesn’t shrink my dreams. It protects them. It helps me focus and keeps my head clear.

Each morning, I ask: 𝐖𝐡𝐲 𝐭𝐡𝐢𝐬? 𝐖𝐡𝐲 𝐧𝐨𝐰?
If it doesn’t serve today’s 3 outcomes, it’s out.
That’s how I stay present with my kids (non-negotiable) and keep momentum on the all the other tracks, even when the “how” isn’t fully clear yet.

Prioritize the present. Move the intent. Keep going.
𝐂𝐥𝐚𝐫𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐡𝐚𝐬 𝐚 𝐰𝐚𝐲 𝐨𝐟 𝐜𝐚𝐭𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐮𝐩 𝐭𝐨 𝐦𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐮𝐦.
I remind myself; success isn’t just the finish. It’s the distance you keep traveling, especially on hard days.

Today’s outcomes included Super Bowl watch party readiness. 𝐆𝐎 𝐇𝐀𝐖𝐊𝐒!

#LifeLessons #WorkDreamLifeIntegration #Enterpreneurship #Parenting #RuthlessPrioritization #MentalHealth #WinADay #StackWins
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about balancing multiple responsibilities, including a full-time job, a real estate business, and family life, and how the author prioritizes their day to achieve their goals and stay present with their kids. The author's approach involves setting clear outcomes for each day and ruthlessly prioritizing tasks that serve those outcomes.

comments.comment_body:
Love Love Love it Swati !! So beautifully articulated. We wouldn't have light bulbs if Thomas Edison decided to give up. And many many such examples. 

Love these two quotes by him. 
"Genius is one percent inspiration and ninety-nine percent perspiration" and "I have not failed. I've just found 10,000 ways that won't work"
comments.post_body:
“𝐇𝐨𝐰 𝐝𝐨 𝐬𝐨𝐦𝐞 𝐩𝐞𝐨𝐩𝐥𝐞 𝐝𝐨 𝐬𝐨 𝐦𝐮𝐜𝐡 𝐢𝐧 𝐨𝐧𝐞 𝐥𝐢𝐟𝐞?"

My six-year-old asked me this during a 30-minute Waymo ride. Instead of talking about productivity, time management, and other loaded concepts, I pulled out my favorite Hindi poem “Koshish karne walon ki kabhi haar nahi hoti.” 𝐓𝐡𝐨𝐬𝐞 𝐰𝐡𝐨 𝐭𝐫𝐲 𝐧𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫 𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐲 𝐥𝐨𝐬𝐞.

It starts with the tiny ant, dragging a grain up a wall. It keeps slipping and falling, but its inner belief fills it with courage, so it returns to the same wall again and again. The effort looks small, but the lesson is huge: if you keep moving, the wall eventually runs out of height. Then a diver, taking repeated dips into the ocean and coming back with empty hands, because pearls do not sit in shallow water. They keep going deeper, knowing that some rewards only appear for people who can handle many empty returns. 

The poem emphasizes 𝐩𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐮𝐨𝐮𝐬 𝐞𝐟𝐟𝐨𝐫𝐭. Failure is a challenge; accept it. See what was lacking and improve it. Give up some comfort, but do not run away from the battlefield of struggle. 

As we reached our destination, I pointed to the Waymo itself. If humanity had not embraced this mindset, we might have stopped at walking. No carts, no cars, no planes, no electric vehicles, and certainly no driverless ride where we can spend 30 minutes on poetry instead of traffic.

To bring it home for him, I reminded him of our hospitality journey. Despite working full time, his parents (Rahil & I) strive to grow our hospitality business Kēsar Vineyards and Events to new heights, not because it is easy but without effort, we will not discover what is possible for our family or the guests we want to serve.

I left him with a question: what is one area you have thought “not for me” that deserves one more honest try this month? Treat every stumble as the ant’s slip or the diver’s empty hand, not as the final verdict.
#growthmindset #grit #parenting #entrepreneurship #hospitality #learning #resilience
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about a parent's conversation with their six-year-old about perseverance and continuous effort, inspired by a Hindi poem, and how this mindset can lead to growth and success in various areas of life. The post shares a personal anecdote about the parent's own journey with their hospitality business and encourages the child to try again in an area they previously thought was not for them.

comments.post_body:
How I built a habit that prevents quitting? 

I developed this as a child. Protect yesterday’s win, then raise the ceiling.

It began in school. Two months without an absence became a quarter, then a year. From sixth through tenth grade I did not miss a day. The streak made the choice each morning. Protect the win. Add one.

That rule shaped bigger moves. I chose engineering and left home after tenth grade to build my own path. Received first US patent, then added more. Same pattern reflected in other aspects. Thirteen years of clean driving record. Sixteen years at Microsoft with strong peer relationships and consistent recognition. The goal was not tenure. The goal was to continuously raise yesterday’s baseline. If 30 pushups is the edge, I do 31. One extra rep turns a ceiling into a floor.

Outside of work, Hospitality Entrepreneurship tested the pattern. Seven years ago we (Rahil and I) converted our garage into a studio and hosted our first guests. Then more condos. Then a vineyard stay. Now we are building Kēsar Vineyards and Events a specialized multi-day wedding venue. Same rule. Keep the last win alive. Stretch it slightly. Then add one to create new personal baseline. This weekend we drove 5hrs to shadow a renowned event and wedding planner during a live event and study the craft up close. New input. New higher standard.

I’m sharing because many of us want a habit that prevents quitting. This is mine. I treat yesterday’s small win as the floor. I refuse to break the floor. Then I add one. I rely on my system: small streaks that become identity, and identity that makes the next action non-negotiable. Tiny on any given day. Transformative across a season.

If you want to try it for 30 days: pick one behavior that matters, set the smallest version you can keep on your worst day, track one visible receipt, protect the floor, then add one. #CompoundingEffort #TheStreakIsTheStrategy #IntrinsicMotivation
comments.context_summary: The author shares their personal habit of 'protecting yesterday's win' by building on small daily streaks, which has helped them achieve consistent success in various areas of life, and invites others to try this approach for 30 days to prevent quitting and build transformative habits. This habit involves setting a small achievable goal, tracking progress, and incrementally adding to it to create a new personal baseline.

comments.post_body:
I’m thrilled to share that I’ve accepted a position at Ford Motor Company as an Engine Powertrain Systems Engineer!

For me, Ford has always been more than just one of the most recognizable brands in the world — it’s a pioneer in innovation, sustainability, and community engagement. Growing up in India, the name Ford carried a sense of heritage and engineering excellence. 

Ever since moving to Michigan, I’ve been inspired by the company’s story — from creating the first moving assembly line to building icons like the Model T, Thunderbird, Mustang, and F-150.

To me, Ford represents the spirit of Detroit and, in many ways, the story of America itself. Joining this legacy feels both humbling and exciting — and I can’t wait to contribute to the next chapter.

I also want to take a moment to thank everyone who reached out after I was laid off earlier this year. Your kindness, referrals, and encouragement meant the world. I’ve learned firsthand that community isn’t just about connections — it’s about compassion. And I hope to pay that forward every chance I get.

For anyone currently job hunting, hang in there. I know how tough it can get when rejections pile up and your inbox is quieter than you’d like (except for that random JCPenney coupon). But there’s always another opportunity waiting — and the right one will find you when it’s meant to.
Here’s to new beginnings, old dreams, and the road ahead at Ford. #weareford
comments.context_summary: The commenter is congratulating the author on their new role as an Engine Powertrain Systems Engineer at Ford Motor Company, responding to the author's post announcing their new position and expressing gratitude for support after a recent layoff. The author is excited to join Ford's legacy and contribute to its next chapter, and is also offering words of encouragement to those currently job hunting.

comments.post_body:
Losing my job after 7.5 years in automotive engineering was tough. But somethings aren’t in your control. This ends a role—not your journey.

Here’s what I’m doing to help me move forward:

•	I am pausing to reflect, not panic. Clarifying what excites me made job searching less overwhelming. I know managing product lifecycles, working with suppliers and launching high quality products is what I love doing. 

•	I am rebuilding my story, focusing on impact and transferable skills. Product management is a skill that’s transferable to any industry. I’m honing my brand and creating a my story that will resonate. 

•	I am investing in learning— Starting my Master’s in AI at Georgia Tech and completing certifications to stay future-ready

If you’re in this phase like me, remember: you’re not starting over, you’re starting from experience. 

Let’s connect—sometimes new beginnings lead to the best opportunities.
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about losing a job in automotive engineering and finding ways to move forward, including reflecting on their passions, rebuilding their personal brand, and investing in new skills. The commenter is likely offering support and connection to someone who is navigating a similar career transition.

comments.post_body:
🎉 Celebrating a Milestone & a New Beginning! 🎉
I’m incredibly proud to share two special moments in my career journey — stepping into my new role as Manager at EY GDS and marking 15 amazing years in this industry and SAP technology.
Reflecting on the last decade and a half, I’m grateful for the experiences, challenges, and incredible people who have shaped my path. This milestone reminds me how continuous learning, perseverance, and passion truly pay off.
As I embark on this exciting new chapter, I’m energized and ready to contribute even more, grow further, and make an impact alongside my new team.
Thank you to everyone who’s supported me throughout this journey — here’s to the next 15 years of growth and success! 🚀

#CareerMilestone #NewRole #Gratitude #GrowthMindset #NextChapter #EY #SAP
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post celebrating a career milestone, where the author shares their new role as Manager at EY GDS and marks 15 years in the industry, expressing gratitude and enthusiasm for the next chapter. The commenter is likely congratulating the author on their achievement and new beginning.

comments.post_body:
Social media attention isn’t worth an ambulance ride.

Last Monday, my feed was full of women on stilettos—holding babies, puppies, even giant rocks.

Let’s be honest: this isn’t empowerment. It’s just a desperate chase for clout.

Nobody posts about the ER visit after a fall. 
Nobody talks about the real injuries.

Is risking your spine really worth impressing strangers online?
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post criticizing the social media trend of women taking risks, such as wearing stilettos while holding heavy objects, in pursuit of online attention and 'clout'. The post argues that this behavior is not empowering, but rather a desperate attempt to impress strangers online, and can lead to real injuries.

After an incredible journey at Articul8 AI — building at the forefront of Enterprise AI alongside a talented and driven team — I wrapped up my time there a few weeks ago.

I'm now pleased to share that I've started a new chapter at Workiva as a Senior ML Engineering Manager, specializing in AI Agentic Frameworks.

In this role, I'll be working alongside Ryan Mensching and Dheeraj Kumar to advance our capabilities in AI agent development. I'm eager to leverage my experience to contribute to Workiva's strategic initiatives in AI/ML.

#Workiva #WorkivaLife #MachineLearning #AI #AgenticAI #Leadership #NewBeginnings

comments.post_body:
💼 After 16 incredible years at Microsoft… I'm moving on.
I’ve accepted a new role as Sr. Director of Engineering at Visa, reporting to the amazing Swati Shukla. [Under the inspiring Rajat Taneja and Harmeet Bala]

Microsoft was the launchpad I could only dream of as a tech-obsessed kid. Joining right after graduating from Manipal (before Satya made it cool 😉), I started as an SDET—getting paid to break Windows. I loved every minute.

A few years in, I realized I was drawn to shaping the what and why, not just the how. With support from [Krishnan Prasad, Anitha Bagyam, Vijay Mamtani, Abhishek Tiwari, Himanshu Soni & Satya Puniani], I transitioned to PM, moved from India to Redmond, and shifted from Networking to Security. Big changes, all at once.

In Windows Security, I worked on Smartcards, Biometrics, and Crypto, and helped lay the foundations of Windows Hello (shout out to Yogesh Mehta, Tony Ureche, Dave Bossio, Sunil Gottumukkala, Janani Vasudevan, Vijay Bharadwaj, Xiaohong Su, Nathan Ide and many more!). I then returned to Networking, leading work across VPN, Wi-Fi, DNS, and IoT, with incredible leaders and teammates (Abhishek Tiwari, Rajeev Nagar, Aaron Cunningham, Vineet Venugopal, Shai Guday, Ravi Rao, Lily Wang, Shahar ., Nikhil Gaekwad, Nick Wood and so many more! ). That phase included several patents (some in the photo below!).

Later, I joined Azure CXP, again working with [Billy Anders] and [Dino Natucci], where we built Community Support and Chat Support, saving Azure over $120M in modeled support costs.

🚀 PRIMO: My Final and Most Impactful Chapter
In PRIMO, with [Oren Rosenbloom, Angie Anderson, Swati Shukla, Sachin Trivedi, Dino Natucci, I helped lead:
★A unified CX Data Platform powering customer insights across Azure
★A widely adopted Observability solution used across engineering & field
★A bold reset of Azure Advisor to support resilient and optimized customer workloads

I’m especially proud of the PM team we built—some of the sharpest, most customer-obsessed minds I’ve worked with— also our engineering partners who brought heart, trust, and world-class execution as well as the amazing UX and Research team.

🔁 So why leave?
Because growth lives outside comfort.
After 16 years, it’s time to push myself in a new environment, stretch in new ways, and grow alongside a different set of challenges—especially as AI transforms how we build and lead.
I leave with deep gratitude to Billy Anders, Angie Anderson, Oren Rosenbloom, Dino Natucci, Sachin Trivedi, Salman Zafar,Matt Wagner 
, Saloni Singh and Sarah Ménard for their belief, mentorship, and support—and to the many teammates, friends, and mentors who shaped this journey.
Let’s stay connected 💙
 Onward and upward.

Instead of the Badge and Laptop, here is a different flavor showcasing the journey!
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about the author's 16-year career at Microsoft, where they held various roles and achieved significant accomplishments, and their decision to move on to a new role as Sr. Director of Engineering at Visa. The author is reflecting on their time at Microsoft, highlighting their experiences, accomplishments, and the people they worked with.

I then returned to Networking, leading work across VPN, Wi-Fi, DNS, and IoT, with incredible leaders and teammates (Abhishek Tiwari, Rajeev Nagar, Aaron Cunningham, Vineet Venugopal, Shai Guday, Ravi Rao, Lily Wang, Shahar ., Nikhil Gaekwad, Nick Wood and so many more! ). That phase included several patents (some in the photo below!).

Later, I joined Azure CXP, again working with [Billy Anders] and [Dino Natucci], where we built Community Support and Chat Support, saving Azure over $120M in modeled support costs.

🚀 PRIMO: My Final and Most Impactful Chapter
In PRIMO, with [Oren Rosenbloom, Angie Anderson, Swati Shukla, Sachin Trivedi, Dino Natucci, I helped lead:
★A unified CX Data Platform powering customer insights across Azure
★A widely adopted Observability solution used across engineering & field
★A bold reset of Azure Advisor to support resilient and optimized customer workloads

I’m especially proud of the PM team we built—some of the sharpest, most customer-obsessed minds I’ve worked with— also our engineering partners who brought heart, trust, and world-class execution as well as the amazing UX and Research team.

🔁 So why leave?
Because growth lives outside comfort.
After 16 years, it’s time to push myself in a new environment, stretch in new ways, and grow alongside a different set of challenges—especially as AI transforms how we build and lead.
I leave with deep gratitude to Billy Anders, Angie Anderson, Oren Rosenbloom, Dino Natucci, Sachin Trivedi, Salman Zafar,Matt Wagner 
, Saloni Singh and Sarah Ménard for their belief, mentorship, and support—and to the many teammates, friends, and mentors who shaped this journey.
Let’s stay connected 💙
 Onward and upward.

Instead of the Badge and Laptop, here is a different flavor showcasing the journey!
comments.context_summary: The commenter is congratulating Aman Arneja on his new role as Sr. Director of Engineering at Visa after 16 years at Microsoft, where he had a distinguished career with various accomplishments and contributions to the company. Aman is moving on from Microsoft to start a new chapter in his career at Visa.

comments.comment_body: Priya Narang Parvathy Krishnan Meera Balsara Mili Patel
comments.post_body:
From Odisha to Microsoft: Celebrating Roots, Resilience & Community This AAPI Heritage Month

As we celebrate Asian and Pacific Islander (AAPI) Heritage Month, I find myself reflecting on a journey that began in a small town (Bhubaneswar) in Odisha, India, in a humble home filled with love, books, and the values of hard work and education. Raised in a teacher family, I learned early on that knowledge is a gift meant to be shared—and that with curiosity, resilience, and community, even the biggest dreams can find their wings.
 
Fast forward to today, I'm proud to be serving as a Principal Engineering Manager at Microsoft—a place where I get to build, lead, and learn every day, while staying deeply connected to who I am and where I come from.
 
Culture has always been my compass. Whether it was celebrating Diwali with homemade sweets and hand-painted diyas, or the simple joy of sharing stories in our native language over chai, these moments ground me. They taught me the power of belonging, and the richness that comes from honoring where we come from—while building something bigger together.
 
This month, I’m especially excited as Asian ERG SVC Bay Area Lead by the amazing work our community has done to bring people together to celebrate food, stories, traditions, and shared experiences. There’s so much beauty in our diversity, and so much power in our community.
 
💬 Let’s make this month a celebration of connection.
- What is a tradition, dish, or cultural value from your AAPI heritage that you enjoy sharing with others?
- Is there an AAPI leader, historical figure, or cultural icon who has inspired you? What about their story resonating with you?
- How has your understanding or appreciation of AAPI cultures evolved over time—either personally or professionally?
 
I’m incredibly grateful to be part of a community that leads with heart, purpose, and pride. Huge thanks to my fellow Asian ERG members Angela Rapadas , Paul Shin, Carolina Bogado Gomez, Vaishali Khandelwal, @Jie Li @Kim Lam and all the passionate individuals (Maha Govindarajan , @Priya Narang, Shorbani Sengupta, @Preeti Naik, @Swetha Joshi, @Mili Patel, Parvathy Krishnan, @Rachana Bhadravathi Shankarnaraya, @Meera Balsara) who make our events, conversations, and celebrations so meaningful. Your dedication, creativity, and commitment to fostering inclusion inspire me every day.

Special shoutout to Aanchal Gupta for her incredible energy, leadership and incredible support for Asian ERG community—you make our culture come alive! And heartfelt thanks to Angela Chen for her encouragement and support—it truly inspired me to share my story. Here's to continuing to uplift, support, and celebrate each other—not just this month, but every day. 💛

#AAPIHeritageMonth #AsianERG #MicrosoftLife #Microsoftbayarea
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post celebrating Asian and Pacific Islander Heritage Month, where the author shares their personal journey from Odisha to Microsoft and highlights the importance of culture and community. The commenter is likely to share their own AAPI heritage, traditions, or inspirations in response to the post's questions and call to celebration.

comments.post_body:
Microsoft is celebrating an extraordinary milestone of 50 bold, groundbreaking, and unforgettable years! Across Microsoft locations worldwide, we are honoring the changemakers who don't merely envision the future but actively build it.

In my own 16 remarkable years at Microsoft, I've had the privilege of working across diverse consumer and enterprise offerings, embarking on thrilling 0-1 journeys, driving impactful growth initiatives, and securing US patents along the way. 

I feel immensely honored and humbled as Microsoft Silicon Valley spotlights my journey as part of the 50th Anniversary celebrations. Back in 2015, when I was based in Bellevue, Washington, I was part of an incredible team that created the first-ever prototype for Microsoft Teams during a spirited Hackathon project. Our vision was simple yet powerful: create a seamless digital workspace to unify people, conversations, and content, making teamwork effortless and efficient. Little did we know then that Teams would rapidly become one of Microsoft's fastest-growing applications in history. It emerged as a lifeline for millions of frontline workers, students, teachers, and colleagues worldwide, especially during the unprecedented COVID-19 pandemic. Today, Teams has evolved into an indispensable part of our daily lives. It continues to grow and now enriched with cutting-edge AI and virtual reality capabilities, further revolutionizing productivity and connection.

I invite you to watch this short video where I share some cherished memories of those early days while hacking at Microsoft. I’d love to hear your favorite Teams moment! #LifeatMicrosoft #Hackathon #MicrosoftTeams #MemoriesforLifeTime #Grateful
comments.context_summary: The commenter is celebrating Microsoft's 50th anniversary by sharing their personal 16-year journey at the company, highlighting their role in creating the first Microsoft Teams prototype and its impact on productivity and connection. They invite others to share their favorite Microsoft Teams moments, reflecting on the application's evolution and significance.

comments.post_body:
A reflection on 2024 

Even though as a recruiter I use LinkedIn on a daily basis, I have barely used it as a medium to connect with the community beyond some job related announcements. So changing that now in the hopes of connecting on a deeper level with the LinkedIn community!

To say that 2024 was like no other year would be an understatement to say the least and here’s why:

I started this year with a huge personal loss, lost my father on the very second day of this year, but I know that he’s in a better place and smiling down on me!

I also started the year by performing umrah (for those who know what it is) with my husband and children and it was truly a blessed experience for us as a family!

My son moved to a different state to start college and I am so proud of him for not just completing his first semester of undergrad with great grades but also dealing with so many things on his own and with so much maturity! So proud!!

On the professional side, had the amazing opportunity to expand my skills and work with a great team at HubSpot! Couldn’t have asked for better leadership and friendship than what I received from Ashley and my partners in all things good Martin and Steph. My journey at HubSpot would not have been the same without all the support from Sarah, Olivia, Melanie, Mark, Becky, Kaitlyn, James, Daphney, Alaina, Linda, Kaleigh,Sarah, Brandon and so many others that made each day so fulfilling for me!! So very thankful for everything and everyone that I got to know at Hubspot this and half of last year!

Starting a new job in the last month of the year is always interesting , now as I am back at Meta, I will admit that I am excited and a little nervous but mostly hopeful to continue the impact that I made three years ago 

Aside from my amazing colleagues and family, I am also so thankful for the friends, acquaintances and my larger LinkedIn community for being there for me over the years and for helping shape up so much of who I am today! I hope to continue to grow with all of you in the coming years!

Happy 2025!
comments.context_summary: The commenter is reflecting on their 2024, sharing personal and professional milestones, including a huge personal loss, a blessed family experience, and new job opportunities, while expressing gratitude for their community and looking forward to continuing their impact. They're responding to their own post about their year in review, hoping to connect with the LinkedIn community on a deeper level.

comments.post_body:
A Lifetime Experience on the Family Fued Stage!

Last week, the show went live! I had the incredible opportunity to play in the Family Feud finale with Steve Harvey. While we didn’t take home the prize, the journey taught me lessons I’ll carry forever - both personally and professionally.

The experience of prepping as a team with my family was a masterclass in collaboration. We strategized, assigned roles, planned our responses, and most importantly, listened to each other. It reinforced how critical it is to think before reacting and how alignment on a shared goal can unlock a team’s true potential.

And then, there was the stage! Debuting on screen was surreal—walking under the bright lights, facing a live audience of 200 people, 5 cameras capturing every angle, and the magic of lights, camera, action. It truly felt like being in a celebrity moment with makeup artists and stylists helping us get ready for our big moment.

But the highlight? Meeting the legend himself, Steve Harvey. Hearing his journey from humble beginnings to stardom was a reminder that perseverance and resilience can lead to incredible heights. I even had the chance to share a bit about my work, my passion for travel, plans for our vineyard and events venue, and so much more. The cherry on top? Dancing with Steve while Harshita taught him some cool moves - it was unforgettable!

It is important to step out of your comfort zone, embrace new challenges, and find joy in the process. Celebrating the journey, not just the outcome is the key!

#Teamwork #FamilyFeud2024  #lifetimeExperience #lessonsLearned #growthMindset
comments.context_summary: The commenter is responding to a post about a lifetime experience on the Family Feud stage, where the author shared their journey of playing in the finale with Steve Harvey and the valuable lessons they learned about teamwork and perseverance. The author's experience is being celebrated as a reminder to step out of one's comfort zone and find joy in new challenges.
